choices.text: Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a SHARED branch. Rebase replaces commits with new hashes (D becomes D'); if teammates have pulled the original D, your force-push of D' creates divergent history — their pulls produce conflicts and confusion. Safe to rebase: your local feature branch you haven't pushed, or pushed only to your own fork that no one else uses. Unsafe to rebase: shared main/develop/release branches, or any branch teammates have pulled. The rule is about ownership of commits — only rewrite history you own.

questions.explanation: Never rebase shared commits. Rewriting commits others have pulled creates divergent history.

questions.prompt: PR has 12 commits — 'fix typo', 'wip', 'try again', 'fix tests', etc. How to merge cleanly?

choices.text: Use 'Squash and merge' on GitHub — combines all 12 PR commits into a single clean commit on main with a meaningful message. The feature branch keeps its original commits (deletable after merge); main gets one tidy commit per feature. Main history stays linear and readable. Pair with Conventional Commits format ('feat: add login form') so the squashed commit drives changelog generation. Alternative: interactive rebase locally to clean up the 12 commits into 1-3 meaningful ones, then regular merge. Squash-and-merge is the dominant 2026 pattern for teams valuing clean main history.

questions.explanation: Squash and merge collapses messy PR commits into one clean commit on main.
